The community's worship — verbal, perpetual, through the mediation of Christ — is itself a priestly act: they are a royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9) offering through the great high priest. The "fruit of lips" echoes Hosea 14:2, where return to God involves verbal confession rather than bulls. Christ is the altar through which this offering becomes acceptable; without him, no praise reaches the Father.
13.16: A revelation of God: do not forget to do good and to share with others, for with such sacrifices God is pleased. The doing of good and sharing are called "sacrifices with which God is pleased" — the ethical life of the community is priestly service. What pleases God is not ritual repetition (the Levitical system exhausted) but the overflow of the atonement's work: love, generosity, practical solidarity with those in need. The God who was not pleased by burnt offerings (10:6) is pleased by the love that the Spirit produces in those who have been sanctified by Christ's one offering and who now live in the grace of the new covenant.
13.17: A revelation of God: have confidence in your leaders and submit to their authority, for they keep watch over your souls as those who will give an account. The leaders who give an account to God are exercising a delegated form of the shepherding role that Christ exercises as the great Shepherd (v. 20). The community's submission to godly leadership is its submission to the Shepherd's care through human instruments. The leaders who watch over souls (psychas) are accountable to the one who gave his soul (psychēn) for the sheep (John 10:11) — all pastoral responsibility is grounded in and answerable to the supreme Shepherd who laid down his life.
13.18: A revelation of God: pray for us — we are sure that we have a clear conscience and desire to live honorably in every way. The author's clear conscience is not self-congratulation but the testimony of a life lived in the light of the atonement — the conscience cleansed by the blood of Christ (9:14; 10:22). The request for prayer is the practical acknowledgment that the community's life together, including the leaders' ministry, is sustained by intercession — the same kind of intercession that Christ himself exercises from the right hand of God (7:25) on behalf of all who draw near through him.
13.19: A theme: I particularly urge you to pray so that I may be restored to you soon. The apostolic desire for restoration to the community reflects the relational structure of the new covenant people: not anonymous worshippers before an impersonal deity but a people bound together in love, mutual responsibility, and pastoral relationship. The mutual prayer requested — community for leader, leader for community — is the horizontal expression of the vertical intercession that Christ maintains for all his people, and in which the community participates by praying for one another.
13.22: A revelation of God: brothers and sisters, I urge you to bear with my word of exhortation, for in fact I have written to you quite briefly. The author characterizes Hebrews as a "word of exhortation" — the pastoral genre of urgent encouragement. All the sustained argument for Christ's supremacy has been in service of this exhortation: hold fast to him, draw near through him, endure for him. The brevity comment is honest: relative to its subject — the pioneer and perfecter, the great high priest, the mediator of the new covenant — every treatment is brief. The letter opens what cannot be closed; it introduces the one who cannot be exhausted.
13.23: A revelation of God: I want you to know that our brother Timothy has been released. If he arrives soon, I will come with him to see you. The personal note about Timothy places the letter in the community of apostolic ministry — the network through which the gospel has been carried and will continue to be carried. The community of Christ is the community of those who share in this gospel-carrying: bound to one another not by geography or ethnicity but by their common Lord and their common commission to speak and embody the word of exhortation to the world.
13.24: A revelation of God: greet all your leaders and all the Lord's people. Those from Italy send you their greetings. The greetings from "those from Italy" — believers sending greetings across distance — signal the catholicity of the community: those in one location are bound to those in another, not by physical proximity but by their common Lord. The community of Christ transcends ethnic, geographic, and social boundaries because it is defined by its relationship to the one Mediator of the new covenant, who has made of many one new humanity (Eph 2:15).
13.25: A direct revelation: grace be with you all. The closing grace-benediction is Christological in content: the grace is specifically the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ — the free, unearned favor of God given through the atonement. All that Hebrews has argued — the Son's supremacy, the high priesthood, the once-for-all sacrifice, the new covenant, the opened sanctuary, the unshakeable kingdom — is summarized in the single word "grace." The community lives in the field of this grace, which is nothing other than the person and work of Jesus Christ given freely to those who draw near through him.
